query: accuracy of diagnosing acute chest syndrome in febrile sickle cell patients using clinical symptoms vs chest x-ray

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Evaluation -- Acute Complications in Patients with SCA
retrieved_docs.content: The risk factors for ACS include a previous history of ACS, asthma, or recent events like recent surgical procedures, pulmonary embolism, fluid overload, infection, etc. The clinical features include sudden onset of cough and shortness of breath. Fever may or may not be a part of the spectrum of presentation. If present, then it usually points towards infection. Laboratory evaluation includes a complete blood count with differential chemistries, including liver and kidney evaluation, blood cultures, and sputum cultures. Chest X-ray shows a new pulmonary infiltrate- this is a quintessential feature of defining ACS. CT and perfusion mismatch scans are only used if there is a strong clinical suspicion of pulmonary or fat embolism. Therefore, they are not usually helpful in acute settings. Sequestration Crises: This can either be hepatic or splenic sequestration.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Chest Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: These criteria lack specificity and can also be diagnostic of pneumonia. Therefore, obtaining a chest radiograph for every patient presenting with respiratory symptoms and sickle cell disease is crucial, as acute chest syndrome can have an insidious start. Early initiation of treatment is essential in managing this condition effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Disease (Archived) -- History and Physical -- Acute Chest Syndrome (ACS)
retrieved_docs.content: ACS is defined as the appearance of a new pulmonary infiltrate on chest radiography accompanied by a fever and respiratory symptoms, including a cough, tachypnea, and chest pain. It is hypothesized that ACS is the result of hypoxia and an inflammatory mediator-induced increase in adhesion of the pulmonary microvasculature to sickled erythrocytes. This process is coupled with a reduction in nitric oxide (NO), which would normally counteract it. The most common symptoms in patients with ACS are fever, cough, chest pain, dyspnea, and lung exam may show reduced air entry, rales, and sometimes wheeze. ACS can progress rapidly to hypoxemia and respiratory failure if not treated promptly. When possible to identify infectious organisms, chlamydia, Streptococcus pneumonia, and Mycoplasma predominate.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Disease (Archived) -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Have a low threshold to obtain chest x-rays to diagnose acute chest syndrome. Get a sonogram of the abdomen as needed. Transcranial sonograms are recommended as screening tests from age two years to prevent strokes. Other imaging studies such as bone scan, MRI, and MRA can be considered depending on clinical suspicion. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Infarction(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis is broad and includes the following: Pulmonary embolism Acute chest syndrome (sickle cell anemia) Infective endocarditis Malignancy Heatstroke AV malformation Cocaine toxicity Diffuse intravascular coagulation Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage Iatrogenic instrumentation, for example, bronchial artery embolization, IV catheter ablation Amyloidosis Pulmonary infection like pneumonia or  aspergillosis Vasculitides

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Chest Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Co-oximetry is the most reliable method for monitoring oxygen levels in individuals with sickle cell disease due to their characteristic shift in the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the right. This shift can lead to underestimating a patient's oxygen pressure when using pulse oximetry and overestimating arterial blood gas measurements.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Chest Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Low fetal hemoglobin (HbF) Young age Presence of asthma or other hyperreactive lung disorder Smoking Recent trauma or surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Crisis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should have a low threshold to obtain chest x-rays to facilitate early identification of ACS. An abdominal ultrasound may be considered for concerns of cholelithiasis. ABG can be obtained for hypoxemia and respiratory failure. [28] CT head and MRI brain would be indicated if there is suspicion of stroke.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Chest Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Screen patients with sickle cell disease for early signs and symptoms of acute chest syndrome. Implement appropriate treatment interventions for acute chest syndrome based on the severity of the condition. Apply pharmacological interventions, such as analgesics, bronchodilators, and antibiotics, as indicated. Collaborate with hematologists, pulmonologists, respiratory therapists, and other specialists to manage acute chest syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Crisis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Predictors of multiorgan failure in chest crises include thrombocytopenia, anemia, an increased respiratory rate, worsening hypoxia, multilobar chest x-ray involvement, and neurogenic compromise. [13] As infection can trigger a chest crisis, checking blood and sputum cultures if needed and nasopharyngeal aspirates for respiratory viruses is best.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease. داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with severe adult respiratory complications, including respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and arrest. In addition, داء الكريات المنجلية is linked to قساح, a term referring to the vaso‑occlusive crisis that characterizes acute painful episodes in this hemoglobinopathy. These interconnections underscore the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt management of crises in patients with sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2353
retrieved_docs.content: Observation and evaluation of newborns for suspected condition not found is a specific form of medical examination/evaluation; it falls under the broader category of medical examinations and evaluations performed to assess health status. This particular assessment focuses on newborns when a suspected condition cannot be identified, ensuring that clinicians systematically observe and evaluate the infant to confirm the absence of disease or to detect subtle signs that may require further investigation. By classifying this newborn assessment as a subset of general medical examination/evaluation, healthcare providers recognize its role within routine pediatric care and its importance for early detection, reassurance, and appropriate follow‑up when a condition is not initially found.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2612
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is classified as a hemoglobinopathy. In the context of this disease, respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is identified as a clinical complication, and the Arabic term الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia) is likewise listed as an allowed qualifier complication. Both respiratory failure and asphyxia are closely linked to نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia), which is a direct manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية. The mapping of الاِختِنَاق to نقص الأكسجين في الدم highlights that low blood‑oxygen levels are a central pathophysiological feature that underlies these serious complications. Recognizing that sickle cell disease can precipitate severe respiratory compromise and hypoxemic states is essential for effective patient monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1667
retrieved_docs.content: SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with a computerized tomography image (صورة مقطعية محوسبة). The imaging procedures CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis, Computerized tomography of chest, and تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي (coronary artery imaging by CT) each have a child relationship with the same computerized tomography image, indicating that these modalities all produce or rely on this type of CT image. This interconnectedness highlights the central role of computerized tomography imaging in evaluating sign‑symptom complexes and in the broader diagnostic workflow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2914
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية). In clinical practice, the presence of chest pain often prompts consideration of cardiac catheterization to evaluate coronary artery disease, while the procedure itself may produce chest discomfort as a post‑procedural symptom. Recognizing this association helps clinicians diagnose underlying cardiac pathology and manage patient symptoms before, during, and after the intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with the الأَشعة السينية على الصَدْر, indicating that this particular pattern of signs and symptoms often correlates with findings observed on a chest X‑ray. This relationship guides clinicians to employ chest radiography when the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is present, facilitating the evaluation of underlying thoracic pathology and informing subsequent patient care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق.
